funFact: Tiger sharks have serrated, cockscomb-shaped teeth that can cut through sea turtle shells — one of the only predators capable of cracking that armor.
description: Tiger sharks are apex predators and critically important for maintaining the health of seagrass meadows and coral reef ecosystems. By preying on sea turtles, they prevent overgrazing of seagrass beds. They are notably indiscriminate feeders — their stomachs have been found to contain license plates, tires, and boat parts.
habitat: Tropical and subtropical waters worldwide; coastal, reef, and open ocean environments
diet: Sea turtles, fish, marine mammals, seabirds, rays, and almost any other prey item

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Sea turtles, fish, marine mammals, seabirds, rays — one of the least selective feeders of any shark

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Low — declining across Indo-Pacific and Atlantic

vitalSigns.label: Key Senses
vitalSigns.value: Highly sensitive Ampullae of Lorenzini and lateral line; olfactory lobes among largest relative to brain size of any shark

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Tropical and temperate seas worldwide; coastal reefs, open ocean, and river mouths

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Surface to ~350 m (1,148 ft); frequently patrols shallow coastal and reef areas at night

populationTrendMeta.note: No direct census exists. Scale derived from exploitation reasoning: tiger shark r ≈ 0.07/yr implies a maximum sustainable harvest of ~3.5% of population. Estimated annual removal of 100–300k individuals implies a minimum population of ~3–8M. The Near Threatened (not Endangered) IUCN status corroborates a large but declining population. Values show ~50% decline since the 1980s, consistent with NT-bordering-Vulnerable status.

preyDeclineRegions.name: Coral Triangle
preyDeclineRegions.note: Sea turtle and reef fish prey severely depleted by overfishing and habitat loss

preyDeclineRegions.name: Caribbean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Sea turtle populations reduced; reef fish stocks declining

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Marine mammal and sea turtle prey under broad commercial pressure

preyDeclineRegions.name: Gulf of Mexico
preyDeclineRegions.note: Prey species under fishing pressure; some seagrass habitat loss

preyDeclineRegions.name: Hawaii
preyDeclineRegions.note: Sea turtle recovery ongoing but fragile; some prey competition

preyDeclineRegions.name: S. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Broader prey base more stable in open pelagic waters

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Finning operations widespread; heavy longline and gillnet bycatch across ocean basins

fishingPressureRegions.name: Australia
fishingPressureRegions.note: Government drum line and shark net programs kill hundreds annually

fishingPressureRegions.name: Hawaii
fishingPressureRegions.note: State-sanctioned culling operations; targeted fishing and longline bycatch

fishingPressureRegions.name: Coral Triangle
fishingPressureRegions.note: Finning and bycatch in tuna and billfish longline fisheries

fishingPressureRegions.name: Caribbean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Some targeted fishing for fins and meat; pelagic longline bycatch

fishingPressureRegions.name: Gulf of Mexico
fishingPressureRegions.note: Longline bycatch in pelagic fisheries; some directed fishing

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing — culling programs and fishing reducing populations

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — wide-ranging behavior buffers localized reef and coastal loss

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining in reef and coastal zones; broad diet provides resilience

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: High — targeted finning, bycatch, and lethal culling programs

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Minimal — few targeted protections; CITES Appendix II

threats.name: Targeted Fishing
threats.description: Fins are among the most valuable in the shark fin trade, and liver oil is used in cosmetics and supplements. Demand drives directed fishing across the Indo-Pacific and Atlantic.

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: Frequently caught incidentally in pelagic longlines, gillnets, and drum lines targeting tuna and billfish. Wide-ranging movements across ocean basins increase exposure to diverse fisheries.

threats.name: Lethal Culling Programs
threats.description: Government-sanctioned drum line and shark net programs in Australia and Hawaii kill hundreds of tiger sharks annually to reduce perceived risk to swimmers, with limited evidence of effectiveness.

threats.name: Habitat Pressures
threats.description: Coastal development and reef degradation reduce prey availability in nearshore habitats, but wide-ranging oceanic behavior provides some buffer against localized habitat loss.

actionItems.title: Oppose Lethal Shark Mitigation Programs
actionItems.description: Government drum line and shark net programs in Australia and Hawaii kill hundreds of tiger sharks annually with limited evidence of effectiveness. Advocate for SMART drumlines, drones, and other non-lethal alternatives that protect both swimmers and sharks.

actionItems.title: Oppose Shark Finning and the Fin Trade
actionItems.description: Tiger shark fins are highly valued globally. Refusing to buy or consume shark fin products and supporting fin ban legislation reduces the financial incentive driving targeted fishing of this species.

actionItems.title: Support Shark Conservation Organizations
actionItems.description: Donate to or follow the work of Shark Trust, Save Our Seas Foundation, and Ocean Conservancy — groups that fund research, advocate against culling programs, and push for stronger international protections.

actionItems.title: Protect Sea Turtle Populations
actionItems.description: Tiger sharks regulate sea turtle populations, which in turn protect seagrass meadows critical to coastal ocean health. Supporting sea turtle conservation — nesting beach protections, bycatch reduction — keeps this ecosystem intact.

actionItems.title: Choose Sustainable Seafood
actionItems.description: Tiger sharks are frequently caught as bycatch in pelagic longlines targeting tuna and billfish. Use the Monterey Bay Aquarium Seafood Watch to choose seafood caught with lower bycatch rates.
